U.S. Strategic Oil Releases Buy Time - But Not Much More

Since the start of the war with Iran and effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, U.S. petroleum inventories have fallen at some of the fastest rates on record, acting a shock absorber to avert fuel shortages and limit price rises.

The United States has become the supplier of last resort, accelerating exports of crude and refined products to the rest of the world, making up for some of the oil lost from the Persian Gulf.

The government owned Strategic Petroleum Reserve has functioned as intended to forestall panic buying and hoarding, minimise the impact on the national economy, and allow additional time for diplomatic and military planning.  

But the rate of inventory depletion is unsustainable, which explains the increasing urgency to find a way to re-open the Strait through negotiations or the provision of military protection to tankers transiting the waterway ...

Goldman Slashes 2027 Brent Outlook to $80

Goldman Sachs cut its average 2027 Brent crude forecast to $80/bbl, from $85, pointing to robust non-OPEC supply growth and structural demand weakness, led by China’s EV push and efficiency gains.

It held Q4 2026 at $90/bbl, noting the Hormuz disruption’s price impact has been milder than expected thanks to softer demand.

Goldman is doubling down on the fundamentals over geopolitics thesis.

Even with ongoing Strait risks, they see supply flooding in and demand softening enough to cap longer-term prices.

Goldman Sachs just cut its 2027 Brent crude estimate to $80 per barrel.

The revision is based on two factors: stronger supply from non-OPEC producers and structurally weaker demand from China.

Goldman estimates that over 10% of China's demand decline becomes permanent as the country diversifies its energy mix away from oil.

This matters because China has been the marginal buyer driving global oil demand growth for two decades. A structural shift changes the long-term calculus for crude.

Brent recently dropped 6.2% in a single session. WTI fell 5.9%. These moves may reflect the market repricing not just short-term supply concerns, but longer-term demand uncertainty.

The contrast is notable: short-term volatility is getting headlines, but the bigger story is a major bank revising its multi-year outlook lower based on permanent demand destruction.

Worth watching whether other banks follow with similar revisions.

Short-term volatility, but the real story is structural demand - watch China.

$USO #Oil #GoldmanSachs #CrudeOil #Markets #FinTok #Investing

🛢️ Goldman Sachs Just Trimmed Its 2027 Brent Oil Outlook, What This Means for Energy Markets

In a notable shift, Goldman Sachs has lowered its forecast for the average 2027 Brent crude oil price to $80 per barrel, down from a previous estimate of $85.

This revision reflects a more cautious long-term view, driven primarily by expectations of robust global supply growth, particularly from non-OPEC producers, meeting softer-than-anticipated demand. Factors like moderating economic growth in key consuming regions, efficiency gains, and the ongoing energy transition appear to be weighing on the demand side, while ample inventories and renewed production capacity ease earlier tightness concerns.

Why This Matters:
For Producers & Energy Companies: A lower price trajectory could pressure margins, especially for higher-cost operators. It signals a return toward structural balance rather than prolonged scarcity, potentially capping upside for oil equities and related sectors into 2027.

For Investors: This aligns with broader market sentiment from institutions like the EIA, which also sees prices easing toward the high-$70s/low-$80s range as supply responses kick in. However, geopolitical risks (e.g., ongoing tensions in key chokepoints) remain wild cards that could spark short-term volatility.

Macro Implications: Cheaper oil supports consumer spending and helps tame inflation, but it may challenge oil-exporting economies and investment in new upstream projects.

Oil markets are notoriously cyclical, today’s bearish revisions can quickly flip with any supply disruption or demand surprise. Goldman’s move highlights how rapidly forecasts evolve amid shifting fundamentals.

What’s your take? 
Will we see $80 Brent hold as the new normal in 2027, or do you expect volatility to push prices higher on unexpected catalysts? Drop your thoughts below 👇

#Oil #BrentCrude #EnergyMarkets #GoldmanSachs #Commodities #Investing

...anything that gets shipped, because $90 oil doesn't stay at the refinery. It moves through everything.

Goldman isn't panicking and isn't revising down. That's the signal. In a week where oil has been pulled in every direction by trade war fears and demand uncertainty, the bank looked at all of it and kept its number exactly where it was.

At $90 a barrel, petrol prices stay elevated. Not crisis-level, but well above the sub-$70 lows we saw earlier this year. The relief at the pump that drivers briefly felt doesn't fully come back.

The hidden pressure is on industries that run on diesel and freight. Supermarkets, logistics companies, manufacturers. Their input costs stay high. That feeds into prices on the shelf, quietly, over months.

Goldman's refusal to cut the forecast is essentially a bet that the world keeps consuming oil faster than it can be replaced by alternatives. That bet has been right for two years running.

[Market Analysis] The Paradox of 'War Fear': How US-Iran Tensions Continually Function as a Stock Market Booster

Geopolitical military tensions between the US and Iran are functioning as an paradoxical engine for stock market boosts in global financial markets. Every time the risk of war hits its peak and subsequently transitions into a cooling phase or diplomatic compromise, the market experiences an explosive 'relief rally'—a pattern that has turned into a recurring mechanism.

1. Escalating Geopolitical Risks: Cooling Overheated Valuation and Creating 'Buy the Dip' Windows
Whenever fears of an all-out conflict between the US and Iran resurface, global indices like the S&P 500 and KOSPI undergo short-term corrections. The injection of heavy macro uncertainties—such as spikes in crude oil and threats to the Strait of Hormuz—effectively flushes out the valuation froth accumulated during prior rallies.
Instead of interpreting this as a structural bear turn, Wall Street and institutional giants leverage the panic as a prime opportunity to "buy the dip." Historical precedents showing that war-driven market corrections typically bottom out within a 10% decline have conditioned large capital pools to stand by, establishing a rock-solid floor under the market during sell-offs.

2. The 'Relief Rally' Effect: Unleashing Suppressed Liquidity upon Conflict Resolution
The core of this mechanism activates the moment military tensions peak and shift toward diplomatic breakthroughs or localized de-escalation. A prime example is the recent sharp drop in WTI crude oil prices back to a two-month low around the $85-per-barrel mark as the Middle East risk premium dissolved.
Stabilized oil prices immediately soothe inflation anxieties and re-open a smoother path for Fed rate cuts. Once the lid of uncertainty is lifted, suppressed investor sentiment detonates all at once, driving a major relief rally that frequently pushes indices well past their pre-crisis highs.

3. The Infinite Loop of Crisis and Resolution Fueling Anomalous Upward Trajectories
Conclusively, the US-Iran war risk has evolved from a catastrophic trigger into a cyclical market regulator that tests downsides and recharges upside momentum.

By suppressing stock prices to cool off overheating and subsequently clearing the air through negotiation phases to invite fresh retail and institutional capital, the global stock market continues an anomalous upward trajectory fueled by the resolution of artificial friction. Strategy experts advise that middle-eastern volatility should be treated as a strict buy-the-range timing signal rather than a prompt for panic selling.
#Geopolitics #US #Iran #StockMarket #MiddleEast #WTI #ReliefRally #Investment #Finance #Macroeconomics #Stocks #Trading #MarketAnalysis

🚨 U.S. SHOOTS DOWN MULTIPLE IRANIAN ATTACK DRONES NEAR THE STRAIT OF HORMUZ

According to Reuters, U.S. forces intercepted multiple Iranian one-way attack drones that were reportedly heading toward the Strait of Hormuz.

Key Details

✈️ Multiple drones were shot down.

🚢 The drones were assessed as a threat to commercial shipping traffic.

⚓ The incident occurred despite ongoing diplomatic progress between Washington and Tehran.

🕊️ It comes as both sides continue discussing a potential memorandum of understanding.

📊 Why This Matters

This headline highlights the biggest risk facing the market right now:

⚠️ Diplomacy is advancing.

⚠️ Military incidents are still occurring.

Both can be true simultaneously.

The U.S. is signaling:

🛡️ Freedom of navigation remains non-negotiable.

🛡️ Commercial shipping will continue to be protected.

🛡️ Threats near Hormuz will be intercepted.

🛢️ Oil Market Implications

The Strait of Hormuz remains the world's most important energy chokepoint.

🌍 Roughly 20% of global oil flows through the region.

📈 Any disruption can quickly impact:

• Oil prices
• Shipping costs
• Insurance rates
• Global inflation expectations

However:

🟢 The successful interception of the drones suggests shipping lanes remain open.
🟢 No commercial vessel damage has been reported.
🎯 Bottom Line
The latest development reinforces two competing narratives:

🕊️ Diplomatically: The U.S. and Iran appear closer to an agreement than at any point in recent months.

⚔️ Militarily: The situation remains extremely fragile, with drone attacks and interceptions continuing even during negotiations.

For markets, the key takeaway is:

The path toward a deal remains intact, but the risk of military flare-ups around Hormuz has not disappeared.

🛢️🌍⚔️📈

Any further attacks on shipping or energy infrastructure could quickly reverse the recent optimism surrounding a potential U.S.-Iran agreement.

"Moving from Middle Eastern oil" - if there is a gap everyone cannot move at the same time.

Crude inventories in the Gulf are increasing as millions of barrels of crude oil a day bypass regulations in the Strait of Hormuz. Traders and ship trackers report the shift. $WTI

President Trump called for the capture of Kharg Island then a peace agreement hours later, whipsawing oil futures during Thursday’s trading session. WTI for August delivery, /CLQ26, settled at $86.16 per barrel, down 2.18 from previous settlement. 

Thursday’s WTI session opened with renewed threats against Iran, sending August futures as high as $91.62 per barrel. In a Truth Social post, President Trump said the U.S. would strike Iran that night and could take control of Kharg Island in the future. Hours later, he announced progress toward a Memorandum of Understanding, which pressured WTI to an intraday low of $84.30 per barrel.

Iran had not confirmed an agreement at the time of the announcement, with confirmation reportedly not coming until early Friday morning. The gap between headlines and confirmation has been a recurring feature of the conflict and may have contributed to reduced market confidence. The volatility also prompted the Senate Agriculture Committee to send a letter to the CFTC requesting a review of recent futures-market activity.

Kharg Island has remained a focal point for Trump given its importance to Iranian oil exports. Kharg is a small island in the Persian Gulf which processes ~90% of Iran’s oil exports, roughly 2.6 million bpd with capacity of 7 million bpd. The capture of this island would cripple Iran’s ability to get their oil to market, however, it would also prompt retaliatory strikes against US military personnel and other oil infrastructure in the region, further tightening global supplies.

A potential agreement may reduce some of the geopolitical risk premium, but supply normalization would likely take time. Inventories outside commercial storage would also need to be rebuilt. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve has declined by roughly 64 million barrels since the start of the year, to about 349 million barrels.

The latest EIA report showed a broad draw in petroleum stocks. Commercial crude inventories fell by 7.2 million barrels to 426.5 million barrels, while gasoline inventories rose by 0.2 million barrels to 215.1 million barrels. Distillate inventories declined by 0.2 million barrels to 102.1 million barrels. Refinery inputs averaged 17.0 million barrels per day, up 80,000 barrels per day from the prior week, with refineries operating at 95.3% of capacity.

Technicals
WTI August crude broke through the 50-day simple moving average of 88.14 but trading well above the 200-day SMA. 

The 14-day RSI settled at 44.63% and indicates a weakening in price as it heads towards the oversold threshold of 30%. 

The directional moving index indicates a strengthening bearish trend. The ADX is low but rising at 11.58. The positive directional index is low and weakening at 17.81 and below the negative directional index of 23.26, which is rising.

text:
◻️Syria - Domestic Service Report - June 12th◻️ 

-◻️Internal Security Forces - Aleppo: After a Syrian military barrier and security point was attacked yesterday in the village of al Auniya and its surroundings in the Ain al Arab region in eastern Aleppo province internal security forces arrested at least 20x individuals believed involved in the attacks yesterday.🔺

◻️WildFire! - Raqqah: A large wildfire broke out in agricultural lands in the al Samra region in Raqqah province with civil defense teams responding to the area to intervene to extinguish the fire from spreading to the neighboring areas.🔺

◻️Traffic Accident - Hasakah: There was a traffic accident involving an oil tanker truck and a passenger vehicle on the road connecting the town of Tal Tamr and the city of Qamishli in northern Hasakah province resulting in the driver sustaining injuries and discharge from the tanker truck contaminated the localized area.

◻️Drowning - Deir Ezzor: Search and rescue operations are ongoing for a young man from the town of Ayyash in the western countryside of Deir Ezzor, after he drowned in the Euphrates River today. Rescue teams and local residents have been involved in extensive search operations since the moment he went missing.

-◻️Counter Terrorism Forces - Daraa: Syrian counter terrorism forces arrested a man named  Qais Hassan Abdul Rajab the former Deputy Director of the General Intelligence Department - State Security Branch with the rank of brigadier general. The General has evaded arrest for some time by moving around often and using intermediaries to communicate.
#Syria #SyAA #Syrianarmy🇸🇾

quotedTweet.text:
The 10.5 Million-Barrel Power Shift: Washington's New Geopolitical Weapon

For the first time in modern history, the United States is the world's largest oil exporter — and it isn't even close. Ship-tracking data from Vortexa, confirmed by Reuters, shows U.S. crude and fuel shipments hit roughly 10.5 million barrels per day in May 2026, the third straight month of American dominance. Russia trailed at 7 million bpd. Saudi Arabia — the petrostate that once dictated terms to Washington — managed just 5.9 million.

Rewind one year: Riyadh shipped 8.1 million bpd in 2025 against America's 6.6 million. That gap has collapsed in five months. Two forces did the work. The U.S.-Iran war, raging since late February, has snarled Saudi flows through the Strait of Hormuz, while Ukrainian drone strikes and U.S. sanctions continue to throttle Russian crude. The Trump administration accelerated the swing in March by releasing 172 million barrels from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. Underneath it all sits the shale base: U.S. crude and liquids output has nearly tripled since 2000 to roughly 22 million bpd.

Make no mistake — this is not a market story. It is a power story. Asia, historically tethered to the Gulf, took some 46% of U.S. exports in May. Europe now leans on Houston more than Riyadh. Even Rosneft chief Igor Sechin conceded U.S. firms were the "biggest beneficiaries" of Hormuz disruptions. The UAE walked out of OPEC on May 1 after 59 years. The cartel's pricing power — the backbone of authoritarian petro-budgets in Moscow, Tehran, and Caracas — is crumbling in real time.

For Washington, energy now joins the dollar and the Pentagon as the third pillar of leverage. For Beijing — import-dependent and watching every chokepoint — the message is unmistakable: the supply chain runs through America now.

Aric Chen Insights
